3: <header>1. Introduction </header>

The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) data show that the global meat production amount has increased by 324% from 1961 to 2012. Particularly in China, this has been the largest producer of primary livestock (32.3 million tons in 2012). The total meat production in China mainland has increased from 9 million tons to 79 million tons over the past 35 years. It is predicted that the demand for meat products in China should be 437.34 million tons in 2020 (Hao et al., 2009). The explosive

development of livestock sector accelerated the debate about whether or not the livestock production is sustainable in the long term? It is known that livestock production appears to have a sig- nificant impact on virtually all aspects of the environment (Steinfeld et al., 2006), for example, much of the estimated 35% of global greenhouse gas emissions deriving from agriculture and land use related to livestock production (Stern, 2006; McMichael et al., 2007); on the other hand, the limited resources, such as water, energy and arable land, restrict the growth of animal prod- ucts, while the global demand for animal products is expected to double between 2000 and 2050 (Steinfeld et al., 2006; Vigne et al., 2012).
Actually, the discrepancy between economic interests and safeguarding of the environment has created ecological problems

throughout the world (Castellini et al., 2006). The issue to design a productive and sustainable livestock production process must be solved. In this context, the development of appropriate assessment methods is essential, as they can serve as decision support tools for guiding the evolution towards more sustainable agricultural pro- duction systems (Hansen, 1996; van der Werf et al., 2007). Various indictors have been proposed to evaluate the economic, ecological and environmental characteristics of livestock production over the three decades, such as the energy analysis (Refsgaard et al., 1998), emergy evaluation (EME) (Castellinij et al., 2006; Zhang et al., 2013), life cycle assessment (LCA) (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Wilfart et al., 2013), ecological footprint (Costa, 2007) and cost-benefit analysis (Maldonado et al., 2005).

EME is an effective method to analyze the sustainability of systems, which addresses the weaknesses of traditional energy analysis and measures variable energy forms using a common physical basis. This method is particularly suitable for evaluating systems at the interface between the "natural" and the "human" spheres (Castellinij et al., 2006; Wilfart et al., 2013) due to the consideration of environmental resource values. Therefore, it has been widely used to assess agricultural systems (Cavalett et al., 2006; Pizzigallo et al., 2008; Lu et al., 2010; Hu et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2014). However, few studies on livestock production sys- tems currently have been assessed with the EME (Vigne et al., 2012), especially for Chinese livestock production. Furthermore, as a donor-sider method, EME addresses the environmental issues or sustainability by identifying the ratio between renewable and nonrenewable flows driving system yet performs not well in ac- counting emissions. This is not a flaw of emergy theory since the theory is derived from natural ecosystems which are considered systems without harmful outputs (Song et al., 2013). However, for the assessment of intensive agricultural or industrial production systems, unavoidable harmful outputs have to be considered due to serious impacts on environment and human health. Ulgiati and Brown (2002) proposed a definition of "indirect environmental service" to calculate the emergy flows of emissions, which was an evolution in emergy methodology since it provided a rational and optional road to evaluate the environmental performance caused by harmful outputs. Nevertheless, this paper just considered the atmospheric emissions instead of other environmental impacts such as water and soil pollution. In other respects, Brown et al. (2012) indicated that indicators of efficiency and environmental performance were fundamental to marking progress more sus- tainable for human development. However, traditional EME indices are unrealistically large or small due to the less natural resources inputs into the process secondary products (Song et al., 2013); meanwhile, the neglect of emissions causes that the indices results just can reflect the sustainability of systems from the input perspective. Ulgiati and Brown (2002) have revised the usual emergy indices due to the consideration of the environmental service required to dilute emissions, but these revised indices did not reflect the impact of the efficiency and sustainability due to the resource type.

Contrarily, life cycle assessment (LCA) is widely taken as an effective tool to analyze the potential environmental impact by identifying emissions of the production chain. Many studies have evaluated agricultural products and process using LCA method (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Chen et al., 2012; González-García et al., 2013). This method, however, is a "user- side" approach, ignoring the contributions of environmental re- sources and labor services. Therefore, some researchers recognize that the joint use of EME and LCA can complement the short- comings of both methods, and this will be very essential to evaluate production systems comprehensively. Based on the idea, a few studies have taken a new synthesized approach linking

these two environmental evaluation methods (Pizzigallo et al., 2008; Li and Wang, 2009; Duan et al., 2011; Song et al., 2013; Wilfart et al., 2013), but most of these studies analyzed the re- sults of EME and LCA indicators separately, which just provided complementary results from different viewpoints. Moreover, some researchers attempted to propose optional frameworks to calculate emergy of products based on life cycle inventories which has been used and modified widely in conventional LCA cases (Rugani and Benetto, 2012; Marvuglia et al., 2013; Arbault et al., 2014, 2013; Iribarren et al., 2014; Tiruta-Barna and Benetto, 2014). Others took efforts to integrate the EME into the LCA as an index to overcome the shortage of LCA on ecological services (Ingwersen, 2012; Raugei et al., 2014; Neri et al., 2014). However, these studies have to challenge the data availability (Rugani and Benetto, 2012). Rather, the study to integrate the LCA into the EME is still rarely reported presently. Bakshi (2000) provided a thermodynamic framework for ecologically conscious process based on EME and LCA, but it was debatable due to use of the emergy to money ratio to account the emergy flows of emissions.

This study, therefore, attempts to introduce the calculation on environmental impact of LCA into the EME to reflect the impact on sustainability of livestock production systems due to harmful out- puts. Furthermore, most of previous EME studies analyzed pro- duction systems as a whole, namely, the "black boxes". This merely reflects the general performance of assessed systems, but the question on which production steps contributes to its higher or lower sustainability remains unanswered. Thus, the life cycle thinking is also adopted in the case to analyze each step of large- scale pig farming to find the key points of optimization of the system. Finally, the revised and redesigned emergy-indices based on the methodology above are discussed. In general, the signifi- cance of this study derives from an improvement of EME based on LCA as well as a supplement of gap on assessment of large-scale pig farming in the North China.

Contrarily, life cycle assessment (LCA) is widely taken as an effective tool to analyze the potential environmental impact by identifying emissions of the production chain. Many studies have evaluated agricultural products and process using LCA method (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Chen et al., 2012; González-García et al., 2013). This method, however, is a "user- side" approach, ignoring the contributions of environmental re- sources and labor services. Therefore, some researchers recognize that the joint use of EME and LCA can complement the short- comings of both methods, and this will be very essential to evaluate production systems comprehensively. Based on the idea, a few studies have taken a new synthesized approach linking

these two environmental evaluation methods (Pizzigallo et al., 2008; Li and Wang, 2009; Duan et al., 2011; Song et al., 2013; Wilfart et al., 2013), but most of these studies analyzed the re- sults of EME and LCA indicators separately, which just provided complementary results from different viewpoints. Moreover, some researchers attempted to propose optional frameworks to calculate emergy of products based on life cycle inventories which has been used and modified widely in conventional LCA cases (Rugani and Benetto, 2012; Marvuglia et al., 2013; Arbault et al., 2014, 2013; Iribarren et al., 2014; Tiruta-Barna and Benetto, 2014). Others took efforts to integrate the EME into the LCA as an index to overcome the shortage of LCA on ecological services (Ingwersen, 2012; Raugei et al., 2014; Neri et al., 2014). However, these studies have to challenge the data availability (Rugani and Benetto, 2012). Rather, the study to integrate the LCA into the EME is still rarely reported presently. Bakshi (2000) provided a thermodynamic framework for ecologically conscious process based on EME and LCA, but it was debatable due to use of the emergy to money ratio to account the emergy flows of emissions.

This study, therefore, attempts to introduce the calculation on environmental impact of LCA into the EME to reflect the impact on sustainability of livestock production systems due to harmful out- puts. Furthermore, most of previous EME studies analyzed pro- duction systems as a whole, namely, the "black boxes". This merely reflects the general performance of assessed systems, but the question on which production steps contributes to its higher or lower sustainability remains unanswered. Thus, the life cycle thinking is also adopted in the case to analyze each step of large- scale pig farming to find the key points of optimization of the system. Finally, the revised and redesigned emergy-indices based on the methodology above are discussed. In general, the signifi- cance of this study derives from an improvement of EME based on LCA as well as a supplement of gap on assessment of large-scale pig farming in the North China.
7: <header>1. Introduction </header>

The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) data show that the global meat production amount has increased by 324% from 1961 to 2012. Particularly in China, this has been the largest producer of primary livestock (32.3 million tons in 2012). The total meat production in China mainland has increased from 9 million tons to 79 million tons over the past 35 years. It is predicted that the demand for meat products in China should be 437.34 million tons in 2020 (Hao et al., 2009). The explosive

development of livestock sector accelerated the debate about whether or not the livestock production is sustainable in the long term? It is known that livestock production appears to have a sig- nificant impact on virtually all aspects of the environment (Steinfeld et al., 2006), for example, much of the estimated 35% of global greenhouse gas emissions deriving from agriculture and land use related to livestock production (Stern, 2006; McMichael et al., 2007); on the other hand, the limited resources, such as water, energy and arable land, restrict the growth of animal prod- ucts, while the global demand for animal products is expected to double between 2000 and 2050 (Steinfeld et al., 2006; Vigne et al., 2012).
Actually, the discrepancy between economic interests and safeguarding of the environment has created ecological problems

throughout the world (Castellini et al., 2006). The issue to design a productive and sustainable livestock production process must be solved. In this context, the development of appropriate assessment methods is essential, as they can serve as decision support tools for guiding the evolution towards more sustainable agricultural pro- duction systems (Hansen, 1996; van der Werf et al., 2007). Various indictors have been proposed to evaluate the economic, ecological and environmental characteristics of livestock production over the three decades, such as the energy analysis (Refsgaard et al., 1998), emergy evaluation (EME) (Castellinij et al., 2006; Zhang et al., 2013), life cycle assessment (LCA) (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Wilfart et al., 2013), ecological footprint (Costa, 2007) and cost-benefit analysis (Maldonado et al., 2005).

EME is an effective method to analyze the sustainability of systems, which addresses the weaknesses of traditional energy analysis and measures variable energy forms using a common physical basis. This method is particularly suitable for evaluating systems at the interface between the "natural" and the "human" spheres (Castellinij et al., 2006; Wilfart et al., 2013) due to the consideration of environmental resource values. Therefore, it has been widely used to assess agricultural systems (Cavalett et al., 2006; Pizzigallo et al., 2008; Lu et al., 2010; Hu et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2014). However, few studies on livestock production sys- tems currently have been assessed with the EME (Vigne et al., 2012), especially for Chinese livestock production. Furthermore, as a donor-sider method, EME addresses the environmental issues or sustainability by identifying the ratio between renewable and nonrenewable flows driving system yet performs not well in ac- counting emissions. This is not a flaw of emergy theory since the theory is derived from natural ecosystems which are considered systems without harmful outputs (Song et al., 2013). However, for the assessment of intensive agricultural or industrial production systems, unavoidable harmful outputs have to be considered due to serious impacts on environment and human health. Ulgiati and Brown (2002) proposed a definition of "indirect environmental service" to calculate the emergy flows of emissions, which was an evolution in emergy methodology since it provided a rational and optional road to evaluate the environmental performance caused by harmful outputs. Nevertheless, this paper just considered the atmospheric emissions instead of other environmental impacts such as water and soil pollution. In other respects, Brown et al. (2012) indicated that indicators of efficiency and environmental performance were fundamental to marking progress more sus- tainable for human development. However, traditional EME indices are unrealistically large or small due to the less natural resources inputs into the process secondary products (Song et al., 2013); meanwhile, the neglect of emissions causes that the indices results just can reflect the sustainability of systems from the input perspective. Ulgiati and Brown (2002) have revised the usual emergy indices due to the consideration of the environmental service required to dilute emissions, but these revised indices did not reflect the impact of the efficiency and sustainability due to the resource type.

Contrarily, life cycle assessment (LCA) is widely taken as an effective tool to analyze the potential environmental impact by identifying emissions of the production chain. Many studies have evaluated agricultural products and process using LCA method (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Chen et al., 2012; González-García et al., 2013). This method, however, is a "user- side" approach, ignoring the contributions of environmental re- sources and labor services. Therefore, some researchers recognize that the joint use of EME and LCA can complement the short- comings of both methods, and this will be very essential to evaluate production systems comprehensively. Based on the idea, a few studies have taken a new synthesized approach linking

these two environmental evaluation methods (Pizzigallo et al., 2008; Li and Wang, 2009; Duan et al., 2011; Song et al., 2013; Wilfart et al., 2013), but most of these studies analyzed the re- sults of EME and LCA indicators separately, which just provided complementary results from different viewpoints. Moreover, some researchers attempted to propose optional frameworks to calculate emergy of products based on life cycle inventories which has been used and modified widely in conventional LCA cases (Rugani and Benetto, 2012; Marvuglia et al., 2013; Arbault et al., 2014, 2013; Iribarren et al., 2014; Tiruta-Barna and Benetto, 2014). Others took efforts to integrate the EME into the LCA as an index to overcome the shortage of LCA on ecological services (Ingwersen, 2012; Raugei et al., 2014; Neri et al., 2014). However, these studies have to challenge the data availability (Rugani and Benetto, 2012). Rather, the study to integrate the LCA into the EME is still rarely reported presently. Bakshi (2000) provided a thermodynamic framework for ecologically conscious process based on EME and LCA, but it was debatable due to use of the emergy to money ratio to account the emergy flows of emissions.

This study, therefore, attempts to introduce the calculation on environmental impact of LCA into the EME to reflect the impact on sustainability of livestock production systems due to harmful out- puts. Furthermore, most of previous EME studies analyzed pro- duction systems as a whole, namely, the "black boxes". This merely reflects the general performance of assessed systems, but the question on which production steps contributes to its higher or lower sustainability remains unanswered. Thus, the life cycle thinking is also adopted in the case to analyze each step of large- scale pig farming to find the key points of optimization of the system. Finally, the revised and redesigned emergy-indices based on the methodology above are discussed. In general, the signifi- cance of this study derives from an improvement of EME based on LCA as well as a supplement of gap on assessment of large-scale pig farming in the North China.

10: <header>2.1. Description of the pig production process </header>

This study takes an intensive pig farming system as the repre- sentative which locates on a large-scale farm (Jinlong Company) in Jing County (37°58'N, 115°99'E), Hebei Province, China. The annual production of pigs in the farm is 100,000. It takes approximately six months to raise a pig from farrow to finish at a mean final weight of 100 kg. All pigs are raised in indoor barns and the pig farming in- cludes the rearing of breeding and commercial pigs. The manure of pigs is pushed into a covered anaerobic lagoon for storage. In this study, the pig production process is divided into seven steps to find out the key points affecting the sustainability of the process based on the "life cycle thinking" (Fig. 1). It should be noted that the data of SOWS and boars on inputs and outputs are average to each commercial pig (Appendix A). All of the raw data are obtained by formal survey questionnaires from the farm manager and worker, on-site observations and measurements from the authors in 2012.
11: <header>2.2. Life cycle assessment </header>

The objective of this study is to assess the direct environmental impacts related to the pig production, so the scope only focused on the pig production and manure disposal stages. The analyzed boundary of LCA and EME for the pig production process in this case can be described as in Fig. 2. The functional unit for the case is 1000 pigs so as to be affiliated with the EME results.
The LCA inventory of the case has two parts: pig production and the manure management. The data of emissions deriving from

combustion of coal are from study (Liang, 2009). Those on the production of manure and the emissions of total nitrogen (TN), total phosphorus (TP), Cu and Zn from manure are from CAAS (Chinese academy of agricultural sciences) and MEP (Ministry of Environmental Protection) of China. The data on other metal pol- lutants such as Cd, Pb, As, Cr and Ni are obtained from Liu et al. (2005). The Data concerning air emissions (i.e., emission factors and coefficients) in the gastrointestinal fermentation of pig and manure management are obtained from IPCC (2006). Ammonia emissions due to the pig manure are estimated according to Liu et al. (2007). Data on the odor emissions from manure are ob- tained based on Zeng et al. (2003).

Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) is the phase in which the results of the inventory analysis are further processed and inter- preted in terms of environmental impacts and societal preferences (Khoshnevisan et al., 2014). In this study, the CML2001 (Guinée, 2001) is selected and seven environmental impact categories related to pig farming closely are involved as follows: water depletion (WD), land use (LU), global warming potential (GWP), eutrophication potential (EP), acidification potential (AP), soil toxicity (ST) and malodors air (MA). Moreover, normalization step is necessary for comparison of the environmental profiles. The normalization factors of world per-capita environmental impact for
the year 2000 (Sleeswijk et al., 2008) are involved to calculate the normalization results in this study.

14: An energy flow diagram of the pig farming system is presented in Fig. 3. It is divided into two parts: the pig production system and the support region. The energy driving systems are classified into three aspects: renewable environmental resources (ER), such as the sun and wind, nonrenewable environmental resources (EN), such as groundwater, and economic feedback resources (F) including pur- chased materials (P) and services (S). Furthermore, the useful yield (YA) and wastes (YB) from pig production are shown on the right- hand side of the diagram.

Actually, the so-called "wastes" are by-products from the pro- duction processes, which participate in biosphere cycles as raw materials from an ecological standpoint. However, from the eco- nomic and social angle on a shorter space-time scale, they are unavailable products and even harmful outputs for the human environment because of their higher concentrations. Many emis- sions into the environment are rendered harmless due to the emergy from services provided by the ecosystem, which dilute or
Fig. 2. Boundaries of LCA and EME on the large-scale pig production system in this case.

Fig. 3. Aggregated system diagram of the large-scale pig production in the case (The red dotted lines indicate the potential emergy flows in the ideal conditions; the black and blue solid lines show the emergy flows from environmental and economic systems in real production conditions; the black dotted lines represent the money flows. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

degrade the emissions to an acceptable concentration or state (Bakshi, 2000). The environment is a source of resources for eco- nomic processes and a sink for by-products from these same pro- cesses (Ulgiati and Brown, 2002). For natural ecosystems, the "source" and "sink" are stable; in other words, the environment can dilute and degrade all emissions from any systems. However, with the onset of industrial civilization, the ability of the environment to dilute emissions of production processes is waning due to the surge of harmful outputs. Under real production conditions, the envi- ronmental service to dilute harmful outputs can be assumed to be a virtual harmless treatment, which indicates that the production system demands sufficient emergy flows to reach a harmless state for the environment. As a consequence, the production system in Fig. 3 shows the real process and the support region that represents the virtual assumption. According to Ulgiati and Brown (2002), the environmental resources (E) driving the system can be divided into two parts: local/direct environmental resources (ED) that are required for the production process, and indirect environmental services (E1) for the dilution of emissions from pig production. In this study, we believe that it will be more accurate to name E1 as the potential environmental services (Ep) because the support region is so limited under the real production condition that the harmful outputs cannot be totally diluted. The LCA can provide the potential environmental impacts of the assessed systems; therefore, the required environmental services should be named potential envi- ronmental services (Ep), which in this study, is just a means to consider harmful outputs in EME instead of real emergy inputs.
15: An energy flow diagram of the pig farming system is presented in Fig. 3. It is divided into two parts: the pig production system and the support region. The energy driving systems are classified into three aspects: renewable environmental resources (ER), such as the sun and wind, nonrenewable environmental resources (EN), such as groundwater, and economic feedback resources (F) including pur- chased materials (P) and services (S). Furthermore, the useful yield (YA) and wastes (YB) from pig production are shown on the right- hand side of the diagram.

Actually, the so-called "wastes" are by-products from the pro- duction processes, which participate in biosphere cycles as raw materials from an ecological standpoint. However, from the eco- nomic and social angle on a shorter space-time scale, they are unavailable products and even harmful outputs for the human environment because of their higher concentrations. Many emis- sions into the environment are rendered harmless due to the emergy from services provided by the ecosystem, which dilute or

The data on the energy content of each item are accounted for using the energy coefficient from Chen (2011) and the formula from Odum (1996). The exchange rate between Chinese renminbi (RMB) and U.S. dollars (USD) used in the paper is 8.17 in 2005, which is suitable for the "emergy money ratio" chosen in the case. The de- tails of raw data calculation procedures are shown in the Appendix

A. The different inputs are organized in emergy evaluation table (Table 4) to convert into emergy by multiplying by UEVs (the emergy evaluation tables of pig farming except for the grow- finisher step are shown in the Appendix B). In this study, the UEVs mostly derive from previous literature and their applicability has been considered and the global emergy baseline used in the case is 15.83 X 1024 sej/year (Odum et al., 2000). Furthermore, the renewability factors (RNFs) of each input have been considered, by which the materials and services that entered the system are divided into renewable and nonrenewable fractions. The approach was considered an evolution in emergy methodology: a significant step forward to a better assessment of a system sustainability based on its renewable emergy basis (Cavalett et al., 2006). The adopted UEVs and RNFs in the case and their sources are shown in the Appendix C.

The ecosystem services required to dilute environmental im- pacts from livestock production can be quantified as the environ- mental emergy necessary to drive the dilution process. According to Ulgiati and Brown (2002), the mass required to dilute the air and water emissions, such as GWP, AP, and EP from pig production, can be quantified with the following Eq. (1), and the mass of soil required to dilute the ST can be quantified with Eq. (2). The volume of air needed to dilute MA is directly equivalent to its characterized result according to the definition of MA environmental impact.

where M is the mass of dilution air and surface water, d is density of air kg/m³) or water (1.00 X 103 kg/m³, W is the volume of air and water in Eq (1) and mass of soil in Eq (2), respectively, which is the amount of a given emission from the pig production process and can be collected from the characterized results of LCA, C is the acceptable concentration obtained from agreed regulations in

China. In this study, the acceptable concentrations are selected according to the national standards on environmental protection released by the MPE in China as follows: CO2 2000 mg/m³, SO2 0.06 mg/m³, TP 400 mg/m³, Zn 250 mg/kg (GBT18883-2002; GB 3095-2012; GB 3838-2002; GB 15618-1995).
2.3.3. Emergy indices

The traditional and revised emergy-based indices are involved in this study. At the same time, we designed two new indices "resource use efficiency (RUE) and unit environmental impact (UEI)" based on the methodology in this case to acquire a more comprehensive view on efficiency, environmental pressure and sustainability from input and output perspectives. On the basis of the different calculation approach, two results derived from the traditional and revised indices were calculated and discussed. A brief description of the emergy indices is shown in Table 1, and the detailed explanation and analysis will be discussed in Section 3.3.

2.3.3. Emergy indices

The traditional and revised emergy-based indices are involved in this study. At the same time, we designed two new indices "resource use efficiency (RUE) and unit environmental impact (UEI)" based on the methodology in this case to acquire a more comprehensive view on efficiency, environmental pressure and sustainability from input and output perspectives. On the basis of the different calculation approach, two results derived from the traditional and revised indices were calculated and discussed. A brief description of the emergy indices is shown in Table 1, and the detailed explanation and analysis will be discussed in Section 3.3.
23: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Environmental impacts and potential environmental services

The environmental impacts of each step on the pig production system are quantitatively calculated and characterized. As shown in Table 2, the WD and LU results for the production of 1000 pigs are 2.63E+06 m³ and 1.49E+03 respectively, and the characterized results for GWP, AP, EP, ST and MA are 9.08E+04 kg CO2 eq., 1.02E+03 kg SO2 eq., 3.54E+03 kg PO4eq., 4.82E+03 kg 1.4-DCB eq. and 7.19E+09 m³ air-eq, respectively. Furthermore, the normali- zation results indicate that aquatic eutrophication is a major
environmental impact related to the production of 1000 commer- cial pigs, followed by soil toxicity and water depletion.

Fig. 4 shows the contribution of environmental impacts for pig farming. All of the categories of environmental impacts derived from the grow-finisher step of pig farming account for greater than 65.0% of total amount, except for the LU, which shows that the percentage of land use for farrowing, nursery and grow-finisher steps are 20.1%, 26.8% and 46.9%, respectively. At the same time, the manure management subsystem contributes to more than 90.0% of the total amount for AP, EP, ST and MA but no contribution to WD and LU (Fig. 4). For livestock production, the loss of nitrogen, phosphorus, heavy metals, odors from the animal manure, water and soil are the main aspects of environmental pollution. The growth of livestock increases the amount of manure produced and causes the AP, EP, ST and MA impacts in the grow-finisher phase of pig farming. Furthermore, the manure management system con- tributes to 59.5% of the total GWP amount, while the combustion of coal for heating the pig house in the winter consists of up to 40.5% (Fig. 4).

Therefore, the potential environmental services (Ep) required to dilute the environmental impacts of the pig farming system are 8.07E+07 J, 3.03E+10 1.43E+13 3.43E+11 J and 1.28E+10 J for GWP, AP, EP, ST and MA, respectively. The results indicate that the greatest amounts of potential environmental services are necessary to dilute EP, followed by the MA, ST, AP and GWP. Moreover, the manure management method is the most important reason for environmental impacts of large-scale pig farming, which is a key point to understand to reduce the environmental impact and improve the sustainability of pig farming. This is especially important for the grow-finisher step because it will greatly

30: As shown in Table 4, the total emergy input for the real pig farming system is 1.13E+18 sej, in which the emergy flows of economic feedback contributes to 99.7% of the total emergy input, followed by the local environmental inputs (0.3%). If the Ep is considered, it accounts for 43.3% of YR. This shows that, unlike planting systems, livestock production relies relatively less on local environmental inputs but largely on purchased resources. This is especially true for the current intensive livestock production sys- tem due to high animal density on a small area. Therefore, the emergy flows of potential environmental services are almost 268.1 times that of local environmental inputs.

The emergy flows of purchased materials and services account for 86.9% and 12.8% of the total emergy inputs. As described in Fig. 5, feed input is the primary portion in the pig farming system, contributing to 77% of the economic feedback emergy. For the

Fig. 5. Emergy structure of economic feedback resources of the pig production system.

different components of feeds, the emergy of vitamin premix ac- counts for 30% (making up the largest item) of the total economic emergy inputs, followed by soybean meal (17%), wheat bran (14%), maize (12%) and concentrated feeds (4%). The building materials, namely, the wood, cement and brick contributes to 6% of the emergy flows of economic feedback emergy, which are the main inputs besides feeds that make up the purchased materials. Moreover, services contain two parts: the direct labor for pig rearing and the indirect labor for the production of building, equipment and veterinary medicine used in the pig farming pro- cess, which contributes 9% and 4% of the economic feedback emergy, respectively. The results above indicate that the feed and labor inputs are the key points to reduce the emergy consumption of the large-scale pig farming system.

From the steps of pig production perspective, we discover that the emergy inputs of the breeding pig rearing and commercial pig farming are 1.86E+17 sej and 9.46E+17 sej and account for 16.4% and 83.6% of the total emergy inputs, respectively (Table 3). Clearly, the commercial pig farming phase consumes the primary emergy of intensive pig production. Although the consumption of inputs and emissions of per breeding sow and hoar per day is more than that of a commercial pig, the input of the breeding pigs is decreased when its average is compared to each commercial pig. Therefore, if a large-scale pig farming system can improve the birth and survival rate of piglets and reduce the non-production days of sows, the emergy flows of the pig farming system will be significantly reduced. Furthermore, the emergy flows of the grow-finisher step accounts for 67.8% of the total emergy flows because this step is four times longer than that of the farrowing and nursery phases and thus the emergy inputs increase. As a consequence, the improvement of the feed efficiency and labor productivity in the grow-finisher step will also be an effective means to optimize the large-scale pig farming system.

9: <header>3.3.1. Efficiency indices </header>

In the traditional EME method, the usual evaluation of the ef- ficiency of the systems is the UEV index: a low UEV means that less emergy inputs are needed to produce a given amount of output. As shown in Table 5, the UEVs of result I and II are 5.44E+05 sej/J and 9.61E+05 sej/j, respectively. The result I indicates the current real production efficiency of the large-scale pig farming system. How- ever, when the potential environmental service is integrated into the total emergy flows of the system, the UEV is increased by 76.7%, showing a reduction of the efficiency of pig farming. This is an interesting discovery. When we focus on the useful products and

inputs consumption from a standpoint of input-output efficiency, we could conclude that the system is good with higher production efficiency. However, this conclusion results in the neglect of envi- ronmental pressure. On the contrary, the revised UEV shows the true efficiency of the pig systems. The reason is that once the large- scale pig farming system wants to stabilize development under the current production mode, the amount of potential environmental services necessary to dilute the harmful outputs from the system for maintaining the environment around it are established. How- ever, only if the environment is healthy can the pig farming system acquire enough feedback power from economic systems to
Table 4 Aggregate emergy flows of each step ofthe large-scalepig production system(sej).

EL: Local environmental inputs including ED and En; Ep: Potential environmental input; P: Purchased materials; S: Services; F: Economic feedback energy (the sum of P and S); R: Renewable emergy flows; N: Nonrenewable emergy flows; Y: Total emergy inputs without Ep; YR: Total emergy inputs with Ep.
Table 5 Results of traditional and revised emergy-based indices system.

Indices|Result I|Result IIb Efficiency indices|Efficiency indices|Efficiency indices Unit emergy value (UEV) (sej|9.61E+05|5.44E+05 Resource use efficiency (RUE) (sej|8.32E+05|NA Emergy yield ratio (EYR)|1.00|1.00 Environmental impact indices|Environmental impact indices|Environmental impact indices Unit productenvironmental impact (UEI) (sej|4.17E+05|NA Environmental loading ratio (ELR)|6.47-|3.23 Sustainability indices|Sustainability indices|Sustainability indices Emergy sustainability index (ESI)|0.15|0.31
a Results I: results with considering potential environmental services. b ResultsII: results without considering potential environmental services (tradi- tional emergy indices).

maintain production. The revised UEV is 76.7%, higher than the traditional UEV, which means that the large amount of emissions is the primary problem that the pig farming system needs to address. Although UEV is a useful index to compare efficiency of systems with the same outputs, it is only sensitive to the conversion effi- ciency of total emergy inputs and not directly affected by alterna- tive resource types (Zhang et al., 2011). The EYR can solve this problem, which is an index to measure a system's ability of using local resources; thus, it can be explained as an efficiency index from the perspective of the input source. Nevertheless, the EYR produces another question: does the higher EYR value mean a higher system efficiency? For many production processes, the answer is absolutely not. EYR is calculated completely based on the amount and classi- fication of inputs of a system, while neglecting the output. The index is effective in evaluating the situation of a system, but clearly unsuitable for a production process in which the production effi- ciency is the primary objective. Table 5 provides an apparent demonstration of the EYR's usability for the evaluation of a pro- duction process: whether the potential environmental service is considered in this assessment, the EYRs are 1 due to a very limited ratio of the local resource inputs. In fact, for any agricultural and industrial production process, the performance of a system in terms of efficiency should be attributed to as least two aspects: the higher transform efficiency of total emergy inputs and nonrenewable resources. A system with a lower UEV and higher EYR is very likely not the one we prefer to use because the higher indices can result from the use of a large amount of local nonrenewable resources. For example, provided that the yield and emergy of the purchased materials and services are not changed, it is possible that wheat planting in the rainy season consumes a large amount of rain for growth and has a higher UEV than the dry season, which uses ground water for irrigation. Wheat planting without irrigation could have a lower efficiency; in the same token, if comparing the wheat production with the same yield, we conclude that the wheat production using more ground water will have the higher EYR, meaning the higher efficiency. Clearly, these results are unreasonable. On the contrary, a good production system should consume less nonrenewable resources to produce a given quality of productivity when requiring less po- tential environmental service due to less emission. However, the current UEV and EYR indices could not solve the issue. Therefore, a consequent index (RUE) is developed in this study to assess the resource use efficiency of the assessed systems, which involves the use of nonrenewable resource and potential environmental ser- vices. As shown in Table 5, the RUE is 8.32E+06 sej/J, reflecting the efficiency of pig production based on the input type and emissions. The RUE index can assess the efficiency when systems with the same outputs have a similar UEV, which is a supplement of the UEV and EYR.

The RUE index can assess the efficiency when systems with the same outputs have a similar UEV, which is a supplement of the UEV and EYR.
11: <header>3.3.1. Efficiency indices </header>

In the traditional EME method, the usual evaluation of the ef- ficiency of the systems is the UEV index: a low UEV means that less emergy inputs are needed to produce a given amount of output. As shown in Table 5, the UEVs of result I and II are 5.44E+05 sej/J and 9.61E+05 sej/j, respectively. The result I indicates the current real production efficiency of the large-scale pig farming system. How- ever, when the potential environmental service is integrated into the total emergy flows of the system, the UEV is increased by 76.7%, showing a reduction of the efficiency of pig farming. This is an interesting discovery. When we focus on the useful products and

inputs consumption from a standpoint of input-output efficiency, we could conclude that the system is good with higher production efficiency. However, this conclusion results in the neglect of envi- ronmental pressure. On the contrary, the revised UEV shows the true efficiency of the pig systems. The reason is that once the large- scale pig farming system wants to stabilize development under the current production mode, the amount of potential environmental services necessary to dilute the harmful outputs from the system for maintaining the environment around it are established. How- ever, only if the environment is healthy can the pig farming system acquire enough feedback power from economic systems to

maintain production. The revised UEV is 76.7%, higher than the traditional UEV, which means that the large amount of emissions is the primary problem that the pig farming system needs to address. Although UEV is a useful index to compare efficiency of systems with the same outputs, it is only sensitive to the conversion effi- ciency of total emergy inputs and not directly affected by alterna- tive resource types (Zhang et al., 2011). The EYR can solve this problem, which is an index to measure a system's ability of using local resources; thus, it can be explained as an efficiency index from the perspective of the input source. Nevertheless, the EYR produces another question: does the higher EYR value mean a higher system efficiency? For many production processes, the answer is absolutely not. EYR is calculated completely based on the amount and classi- fication of inputs of a system, while neglecting the output. The index is effective in evaluating the situation of a system, but clearly unsuitable for a production process in which the production effi- ciency is the primary objective. Table 5 provides an apparent demonstration of the EYR's usability for the evaluation of a pro- duction process: whether the potential environmental service is considered in this assessment, the EYRs are 1 due to a very limited ratio of the local resource inputs. In fact, for any agricultural and industrial production process, the performance of a system in terms of efficiency should be attributed to as least two aspects: the higher transform efficiency of total emergy inputs and nonrenewable resources. A system with a lower UEV and higher EYR is very likely not the one we prefer to use because the higher indices can result from the use of a large amount of local nonrenewable resources. For example, provided that the yield and emergy of the purchased materials and services are not changed, it is possible that wheat planting in the rainy season consumes a large amount of rain for growth and has a higher UEV than the dry season, which uses ground water for irrigation. Wheat planting without irrigation could have a lower efficiency; in the same token, if comparing the wheat production with the same yield, we conclude that the wheat production using more ground water will have the higher EYR, meaning the higher efficiency. Clearly, these results are unreasonable. On the contrary, a good production system should consume less nonrenewable resources to produce a given quality of productivity when requiring less po- tential environmental service due to less emission. However, the current UEV and EYR indices could not solve the issue. Therefore, a consequent index (RUE) is developed in this study to assess the resource use efficiency of the assessed systems, which involves the use of nonrenewable resource and potential environmental ser- vices. As shown in Table 5, the RUE is 8.32E+06 sej/J, reflecting the efficiency of pig production based on the input type and emissions. The RUE index can assess the efficiency when systems with the same outputs have a similar UEV, which is a supplement of the UEV and EYR.

15: Ulgiati et al. (2006) argued that EME pertained to upstream impact indicators, which was concerned with the inputs and accounted for the depletion of environmental resources. The concept of environmental pressure of EME implies that if an envi- ronmental resource is used by a process, then it is not available for another process (Cavalett et al., 2006). The ELR was built according to this connation and is the typical index used to assess environ- mental pressure in the traditional EME. However, the index only reflects the characteristic of resource inputs without considering emissions. This is not a weakness of the EME because it is an up- stream method, but the neglect for harmful outputs has been recently criticized when regarding the systems that produce a large amount of emissions. In this study, the revised ELR is 6.47, which is 100.3% higher than the one without considering the potential environmental services (3.23). This result demonstrates that the emissions of the pig farming system indeed result in serious environmental pressure. Clearly, it is important for the evaluation of environmental pressure to consider harmful outputs in EME.

Furthermore, the UEI in this case is proposed to evaluate the environmental pressure of the system from the output perspective, which reflects the potential environmental service required to produce the useful output of the unit. The higher the UEI value, the higher the environmental emissions generated by the unit available output. In this case, the UEI is 4.17E+05 sej/J (Table 5). This result can be viewed as the environmental impact result of LCA in the terms of emergy. The joint use of UEI and ELR can effectively assess the environmental pressure of the production process from the input and output standpoints.

17: <header>3.3.3. Sustainability indices </header>

To some extent, the sustainability of a system reflects its comprehensive performance of system efficiency and environ- mental impacts. A sustainable system will cause less environmental impacts to maintain the supply of resources, and at the same time, produce more outputs to contribute to the environment and strengthen the self-organization ability of the assessed system by the increased feedback energy. In a comparison of the ESI value (0.31) calculated according to the traditional EME, the revised ESI (0.15) decreases by 51.6% (Table 4), which reflected the impact of emissions on the sustainability of the pig farming system.
18: <header>4. Discussion </header>
4.1. Complementarity between LCA and EME

EME is particularly suitable for agricultural studies because farming is a system in which natural and human contributions interact to generate a final product such as primary production (Lagerberg and Brown, 1999). However, for various productive processes of secondary products and the intensive pig farming system in this study, the traditional EME shows clear limits when assessing the sustainability of these production processes due to insufficient consideration of harmful outputs. The combination of LCA and EME enables a broader view of the effects of a system on its environment (Wilfart et al., 2013). EME shows how to maximize resource use while LCA allows identification of where to reduce pollutant emissions and improve the reuse of wastes and quantifies the related benefits (Rugani and Benetto, 2012). Actually, the sim- ilarity between LCA and EME existence is essential. EME contains an inner "life cycle" concept because it reflects the "memory pro- cess" of energy. The key parameters, UEVs and RNFs, in the EME are calculated by the direct and indirect inputs of solar emergy, or renewable and nonrenewable resources, and thus, when account- ing for the inputs of emergy of a system by multiplying the raw data

with the parameters, we acquire the solar energy regarding the goods and services of the environment and economy from the formation to the use of the resources. This is actually a life cycle connotation. Nevertheless, the combination of LCA and EME also faces certain challenges, such as:

21: <header>(1) Standpoint </header>

EME is considered a "donor-side" method, which is substantially rooted in the direct and indirect consumption of environmental resources, while LCA is a "human-oriented" approach. The differ- ence may result in confusion for the combination of the methods, but alternatively, it may be a valuable supplement. Some re- searchers have attempted to integrate the donor-side standpoint of EME into LCA due to the lack of consideration of environmental resources and ecosystem services of the latter. Hua and Bakshi (2004) proposed the ecological cumulative exergy consumption (ECEC) index in the life cycle assessment. Defined the Solar Energy Demand (SED) to calculate the life cycle inventory. Zhang et al. (2010) proposed an Eco-LCA model, which included ecosystem services as inputs into a life cycle model at the process or economy scale. These concepts are equivalent with emergy if the boundaries of the study, including the allocation approach and the method for combining global energy inputs, are identical (Rugani and Benetto, 2012). Clearly, this unique perspective leads to a natural expansion of the LCA boundary to include the energy upstream of LCA (Raugei et al., 2014). Conversely, the user-side perspective of LCA can also be integrated into the EME when accounting emissions derived from the evaluated system, such as the potential environmental services calculation in this case.
22: <header>(2) System boundary </header>

An appropriate system boundary is the foundation of an assessment, but the boundaries of LCA and EME are different. First, EME attempts to evaluate the energy memory associated with in- formation and labor services as well as ecosystem services such as rain, tides and wind, while LCA does not generally consider these inputs because they are assumed to be freely available and not scarce (Rugani and Benetto, 2012). Second, EME only pays attention to the direct and useful products, such as the pig in this case, while LCA only considers indirect and harmful products. Third, the emergy accounting of nonrenewable resources, such as petroleum, groundwater and coal, is extended back in time to include the environmental work needed for resource formation (Ulgiati et al., 2006). Fig. 2 in the present case is a visual comparison of system boundaries between the EME and LCA.
23: <header>(3) Available data </header>

The UEVs and RNFs are very important in emergy accounting, but the standard database is still unavailable. This limit causes inaccu- racy of the emergy evaluation, which is also the primary issue criticized by some researchers. Odum and colleagues have accoun- ted for some basic parameters (Odum, 1996; Odum et al., 2000), but the covered materials are limited. Therefore, researchers must choose appropriate UEVs and RNFs from various studies or estimate the data of input systems when they evaluate certain complex systems, and this increases the uncertainty of the results. Recently, Rugani and co-authors attempted to introduce the life cycle in- ventory databases into the EME method to calculate the UEV of resources and outputs (Rugani and Benetto, 2012). At the same time, they developed a software named SCALE (Software for CALculating Emergy based on life cycle inventories), which is the first tool using

an LCI database for automatic emergy calculation in compliance with the emergy algebra rules (Marvuglia et al., 2013; Arbault et al., 2014). This is a great step forward for integration of the LCA into the EME and to improve the availability and accuracy of EME; never- theless, the problem of double-counting still exists. Furthermore, most of the LCI databases were collected based on the models and records of production processes in developed countries, and thus, researchers in China should develop a local database to integrate into emergy accounting for the evaluation of cases in China.
24: <header>(3) Available data </header>

The UEVs and RNFs are very important in emergy accounting, but the standard database is still unavailable. This limit causes inaccu- racy of the emergy evaluation, which is also the primary issue criticized by some researchers. Odum and colleagues have accoun- ted for some basic parameters (Odum, 1996; Odum et al., 2000), but the covered materials are limited. Therefore, researchers must choose appropriate UEVs and RNFs from various studies or estimate the data of input systems when they evaluate certain complex systems, and this increases the uncertainty of the results. Recently, Rugani and co-authors attempted to introduce the life cycle in- ventory databases into the EME method to calculate the UEV of resources and outputs (Rugani and Benetto, 2012). At the same time, they developed a software named SCALE (Software for CALculating Emergy based on life cycle inventories), which is the first tool using

an LCI database for automatic emergy calculation in compliance with the emergy algebra rules (Marvuglia et al., 2013; Arbault et al., 2014). This is a great step forward for integration of the LCA into the EME and to improve the availability and accuracy of EME; never- theless, the problem of double-counting still exists. Furthermore, most of the LCI databases were collected based on the models and records of production processes in developed countries, and thus, researchers in China should develop a local database to integrate into emergy accounting for the evaluation of cases in China.
25: <header>(4) Evaluation indices </header>

The joint and hybrid analysis of LCA and EME have been encouraged in recent years and have been applied to various fields, such as wine production (Pizzigallo et al., 2008), urban residential areas (Li and Wang, 2009), wetland parks (Duan et al., 2011), aquaculture farming (Wilfart et al., 2013) and e-waste treatment (Song et al., 2013), but the hybrid degree of these studies are different. Most relative studies analyzed the resource consumption by EME indices and environmental impacts by LCA results sepa- rately, while others attempted to design new indices to integrate the results of EME and LCA such as the Emergy-LCA sustainability index (ELSI) (Song et al., 2013) and Emergy margin environmental impact index (Li and Wang, 2009). Similarly, in this paper, the au- thors attempted to design new indices (RUI and UEI) to combine the results from LCA and EME. The usual emergy-indices some- times cannot reflect the real characteristics of production processes and their generation of secondary products. For example, the EYR was 1.00 in this paper whether it was calculating the potential environmental service. Brown et al. (2012) identified a common framework to account for EYR and ELR indices in emergy synthesis based on the data categories of foreground and background in LCA. Arbault et al. (2014) further analyzed the implications of the di- versity of definitions and formulations of EYR and ELR proposed in the emergy methodology, with regard to the mathematical decomposition of ESI. Regardless of the creation of new indices and the revision of the original indices, the integrations based on LCA and EME are very necessary to improve the ability of EME to evaluate production processes.
26: <header>4.2. System optimization strategy </header>

The assessment results of the traditional EME and LCA methods generally result in limited choices for an optimization strategy of the systems due to the differences in the basic objectives of these two methods. Policy makers choose to either ignore the ecosystem service demands or the potential environmental impacts of the assessed system according to the separated evaluation results. Thus, the optimization strategies sometimes ameliorate one point but deteriorate another point. According to the separate EME re- sults in this paper, the sustainability of the system will be improved if natural firewood around the farm is used to replace electricity for heating of the pig houses in winter because the firewood has a lower UEV (7.39E+04sej/J) (Jiang et al., 2007) and a higher RNF (1.00) than that of electricity. Nevertheless, the firewood would increase the direct emission of greenhouse gases of the process from the LCA perspective. Clearly, the strategy based on a single- criterion will not optimize the evaluated system comprehensively. In light of the introduction of potential environmental services based on LCA in the study, the resource consumption and direct emissions are both considered, and the concluded optimization strategy is therefore more comprehensive. Another important foundation of optimization is the need to diagnose the key points impacting the assessed process. One of the

strong points of LCA is the analysis of the production chain of goods from cradle to tomb (or to gate), which assists in discovering the regulation phase to reduce the environmental impacts derived from the product. The idea of "life cycle thinking" is very useful for the optimization of evaluated systems because it can help to determine the key step within the system, and then a specific technology can be used to improve the performance of the system. Some EME studies have made efforts in a similar respect. Cavalett and Ortega (2009) evaluated the whole chain of soybean produc- tion and industrial processing stages in Brazil; Cuadra and Rydberg (2006) also analyzed the coffee production, processing and export phases in Nicaragua. Nevertheless, these studies did not unpack the "black box" of every production step to determine the detailed regulation points. In this study, the analysis at each step of large- scale pig farming provides ways to maximize efficiency and reduce emissions at the key points. As reported, the grow-finisher step consumed 68.5% of total emergy flows, which is a key step that needs to be regulated. The efficiency and sustainability of this step are affected primarily by the feed inputs and potential envi- ronmental services due to manure management. Therefore, if the two parts in the grow-finisher step are improved by appropriate technologies, such as the feed composition and biogas projects, the emergy efficiency and sustainability of pig production will be significantly increased.

28: <header>5. Conclusions </header>

The steady growth of the world's economy and population, especially in developing countries, is increasing the demand for livestock products. However, under the current background of a shortage of resources and environmental pollution, we hope to in- crease yields and reduce resource consumption and environmental impacts of livestock production. To some extent, the sustainability of production processes will be the final and fundamental target for governments to construct an environmentally friendly society and economy and is the common goal of humans all over the world. Therefore, a comprehensive evaluation is necessary for the devel- opment of sustainable pig farming in China.

In this study, the calculation of environmental impacts using the LCA method is integrated into the traditional EME by the definition of potential environmental services. Seven environmental impacts, such as global warming potential, eutrophication potential, acidi- fication potential and soil toxicity, are considered. The "life cycle" idea is also introduced into the study to diagnose the key points of system optimization. The combination of the two methods enables a broader view of the sustainability of a system. The revised and created indices showed that the large-scale pig farming system has a low level of sustainability. When the harmful outputs of pig farming are considered, the assessment results showed a signifi- cant reduction in sustainability compared to the real production situation. Apparently, the harmful outputs from the livestock pro- duction system must be taken into account when evaluating the system because the sustainability of the evaluated system is significantly affected by these factors. At the same time, the tradi- tional emergy indices represent limits for evaluating efficiency and environmental performance of a production process, and the designed indices of RUE and UEI, which are based on the joint use of LCA and EME in this case, seem to be effective as a supplement. Nevertheless, the joint use of EME and LCA is still important to develop new and revised indices to improve sustainability evalu- ation of production processes from various viewpoints. Further- more, the results showed that in terms of the pig life cycle, the improvement of feed composition and labor productivity in the

grow-finisher step are the most important points for the optimi- zation of the large-scale pig farming system. An effective optimi- zation strategy will also improve the birth and survival rate of piglets and reduce the non-production days of sows.
